name: file-path-traversal
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to \"test for directory traversal\", \"exploit path traversal vulnerabilities\", \"read arbitrary files through web applications\", \"find LFI vu...
capabilities: Identify potential path traversal injection points in web application parameters
Generate and test path traversal payloads with various encoding schemes
Document discovered vulnerabilities with proof-of-concept evidence
Provide remediation guidance based on secure coding practices
Test bypass techniques for common input validation filters
Assess impact by identifying accessible sensitive files
author: zebbern
securitySummary: This is a legitimate security testing skill for penetration testers. Static analysis detected 246 patterns, but all are code examples within educational documentation, not executable malicious code. The skill provides testing methodologies for path traversal vulnerabilities (CVE-2021-44228 style testing), including bypass techniques, target file lists, and prevention measures. Content is appropriate for security professionals with proper authorization.
valueStatement: Web applications often expose sensitive files through path traversal vulnerabilities. This skill provides security professionals with comprehensive testing methodologies to identify and document these security gaps before attackers exploit them.
userTitle: Test web applications for path traversal vulnerabilities
limitations: Only tests for path traversal vulnerabilities - does not cover other vulnerability types
Requires user to have proper authorization and legal permission to test target systems
Cannot bypass network-level protections or firewalls automatically
Effectiveness depends on application responses and error message disclosure

useCases.title: Penetration Testing Engagement
useCases.description: Security consultants testing web applications for clients need systematic path traversal testing to fulfill OWASP Top 10 coverage requirements and deliver comprehensive vulnerability assessments.

useCases.title: Security Code Review
useCases.description: Development teams reviewing legacy code or third-party components can use this skill to identify path traversal vulnerabilities before deployment and implement proper input validation.

useCases.targetUser: Bug bounty hunters and security researchers
useCases.title: Bug Bounty Hunting
useCases.description: Independent security researchers participating in bug bounty programs can systematically test for path traversal vulnerabilities across multiple targets with documented methodologies.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Path Traversal Test
promptTemplates.prompt: Test the endpoint [ENDPOINT_URL] with parameter [PARAMETER_NAME] for path traversal vulnerabilities. Start with basic payloads like ../../../etc/passwd and document any responses that differ from normal behavior.
promptTemplates.scenario: Initial vulnerability assessment of a file download feature

promptTemplates.title: Encoded Payload Testing
promptTemplates.prompt: The application blocks ../ sequences. Generate URL-encoded and double-encoded path traversal payloads to test filter bypass. Include variations like %2e%2e%2f and ..%252f patterns.
promptTemplates.scenario: Testing against input validation that strips traversal sequences

promptTemplates.title: LFI to RCE Escalation
promptTemplates.prompt: We have confirmed LFI on [ENDPOINT]. Test escalation to remote code execution using log poisoning or PHP wrapper techniques. Document each attempt and response for the vulnerability report.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced exploitation after confirming initial vulnerability

promptTemplates.title: Remediation Report Generation
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a remediation report for discovered path traversal vulnerabilities. Include root cause analysis, secure code examples in [LANGUAGE], and references to OWASP guidelines.
promptTemplates.scenario: Delivering findings to development team for remediation

bestPractices: Always obtain written authorization before testing any system you do not own
Document all testing activities with timestamps, payloads used, and responses received
Limit testing to minimum necessary to confirm vulnerability - avoid exfiltrating sensitive data
Follow responsible disclosure practices when reporting findings to stakeholders
antiPatterns: Testing production systems without explicit written authorization from the owner
Exfiltrating sensitive data (credentials, PII) beyond what is needed to prove the vulnerability
Using automated tools at high volume that could cause denial of service or data corruption
Sharing vulnerability details publicly before the organization has time to remediate

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: This skill is legal when used on systems you own or have explicit written authorization to test. Unauthorized security testing may violate computer crime laws. Always obtain proper authorization before testing.

faq.question: What is the difference between LFI and path traversal?
faq.answer: Local File Inclusion (LFI) is a specific type of path traversal where the application includes local files, often leading to code execution. Path traversal is broader - accessing files outside the intended directory. LFI is typically more severe.

faq.question: Can this skill test for remote file inclusion (RFI)?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on local path traversal. RFI (including remote URLs) is a related but distinct vulnerability. The techniques overlap, but RFI requires different payloads targeting URL inclusion.

faq.question: What should I do if I find a path traversal vulnerability?
faq.answer: Document the vulnerability with proof (sanitized), assess the impact, and report it to the system owner through their responsible disclosure process. Do not exploit beyond confirming the vulnerability exists.

faq.question: Why do some payloads use null bytes (%00)?
faq.answer: Null bytes can truncate strings in older PHP versions (before 5.3.4), bypassing extension checks like .jpg. This is less effective on modern systems but included for completeness in testing legacy applications.

faq.question: How do I know if my testing is causing harm?
faq.answer: Monitor response times and error rates. If you see 500 errors, timeouts, or service degradation, stop testing immediately. Path traversal reads are generally safe, but avoid testing write/delete functionality on production systems.
